metadata.title: Why telling Claude "you are a logic bot" turns a wrong answer right
metadata.topic: Role text that changes correctness, not just tone
metadata.purpose: A role primes a whole cluster of context and behavior — priming the logic-bot role pulls Claude toward careful step-through reasoning it skipped by default, so role prompting can lift performance on math and logic, not just tone.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: system-prompt vs user-turn placement details, audience priming
metadata.visual_object: One logic problem with two persona badges — default (wrong answer) and "logic bot" (right answer)
metadata.hook: A role assignment reads like a style choice — voice, tone, persona — yet on a reasoning problem it can move Claude from a wrong answer to a right one.

beats.narration_text: Given a logic problem with a definite "yes," Claude says it lacks information and gets it wrong. Prime it with "You are a logic bot" and, on the same problem, it answers correctly.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: A role is supposed to shape style, not truth; here it fixed a reasoning failure; why should "act as a logic bot" change whether Claude is correct?
A role assignment reads like a style choice — voice, tone, persona — yet on a reasoning problem it can move Claude from a wrong answer to a right one.

A role primes a whole cluster of context and behavior — priming the logic-bot role pulls Claude toward careful step-through reasoning it skipped by default, so role prompting can lift performance on math and logic, not just tone.
beats.visual: One logic problem with two persona badges — default (wrong answer) and "logic bot" (right answer)
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: "Is the conclusion valid?" Default → "not enough info" (wrong). Logic-bot role → "yes, valid" (right).

A role primes a whole cluster of context and behavior — priming the logic-bot role pulls Claude toward careful step-through reasoning it skipped by default, so role prompting can lift performance on math and logic, not just tone.
